worth_it_explanation: Floor pricing sets the price below which an impression goes unsold, so it controls the trade between fill rate and the value of each impression. A floor that leans on one flat number across every placement, format, country, and device, or one that accepts a dynamic floor without measuring it against a fixed baseline, can leave revenue uncollected without anyone noticing. The check is a review of the pricing rules a publisher already has in Microsoft Monetize, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete pricing decision the publisher can test.
intro: Floor pricing decides the minimum price Microsoft Monetize accepts for an impression, which sets where the line falls between filling more impressions and protecting the value of each one. This subcategory is about the floor strategy itself, the values chosen and how they are applied, rather than the mechanics of the pricing controls that carry them. These checks confirm that the floors are a deliberate, evidence-based choice rather than a single flat number or an automated floor accepted by default, and that they reflect the price differences across the publisher's inventory.

audit_checks.title: Floors are not segmented by inventory, format, geography, or device
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A single flat floor across all inventory ignores that some placements, formats, countries, and devices clear at higher prices than others. A flat floor either underprices the strong inventory, leaving revenue uncollected, or overprices the weak inventory, leaving impressions unsold, so one number cannot serve inventory whose value varies along several lines at once.

educate.base:
A floor price works best when it matches what the impression is worth, and impressions are not worth the same across a publisher's inventory. An above-the-fold unit on a high-engagement page clears higher than a unit far down a thin page. A video impression clears higher than a small banner. Traffic from a high-spending country clears higher than traffic from a market where advertiser demand is thin. Desktop, mobile web, and in-app inventory each carry their own demand and their own typical clearing price. A floor set as one flat number across all of this cannot fit inventory whose value varies along every one of those lines.

The cost of a flat floor cuts both ways. Set it to the average and it sits below the strong inventory, so impressions that could have cleared higher clear at the floor and the difference is left uncollected. Set it higher to protect the strong inventory and it sits above the weak inventory, so impressions that would have sold at a lower price go unsold instead. Either way, one number serves the middle and misprices the ends.

Microsoft Monetize is a sell-side platform built on the AppNexus stack, and it lets a publisher set floors that target the dimensions which move clearing price, so different floors can apply to different placements, formats, geographies, and device types rather than one floor for everything. Because Microsoft Monetize is Prebid-compatible, the same segmentation logic that the Prebid Price Floors module describes (floor rules keyed by media type, size, and other dimensions) maps onto how floors are reasoned about here.

A few signs point to floors that are not segmented:
  • One floor value covers all inventory, with no breakout by placement, format, geography, or device
  • Reporting shows average clearing prices that differ widely across placements, formats, countries, or devices while the floor stays the same for all of them
  • Premium placements clear at or near the same floor as filler inventory, which suggests the floor is not protecting their value
You may want to confirm that the floors reflect the price differences the inventory actually shows. One approach is to read average clearing price by placement, format, geography, and device, then set floors that follow those differences rather than a single number across the board.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a homepage or section-front placement usually clears higher than an article-footer unit, video clears higher than a small banner, and audience from high-demand markets clears higher than audience from thin ones. You may want to set floors by placement, format, and geography so the premium inventory holds its value while the lower-value inventory keeps a floor low enough to stay filled.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, product and category pages reach an audience closer to a purchase and clear higher than editorial pages, and device matters because mobile shoppers and desktop shoppers carry different demand. You may want to segment floors so the commercial, higher-intent inventory carries a higher floor than the editorial inventory rather than sharing one number.

learn_more.source_title: Price Floors for Header Bidding (floor rules by media type, size, and dimension)
OpenRTB (bidfloor signaling between seller and buyer)

educate.learn_more_note: Microsoft Monetize's detailed configuration documentation for setting and segmenting floors is in the Microsoft Advertising partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the floor values across placements, formats, geographies, and device types to see whether they are differentiated or whether one flat floor covers everything.

monetize_console.label: Microsoft Monetize console
steps: Open the seller's floor or pricing configuration and list the active floor rules with their values.
For each rule, note which placements, ad formats, geographies, and device types it covers and at what floor.
Record whether the floors differ across placement, format, geography, and device, or whether a single floor applies across all inventory with no breakout.

monetize_reporting.label: Microsoft Monetize reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by placement, then by ad format, then by country, then by device type, with average eCPM and fill rate.
Compare the average clearing price across those dimensions to see how much it varies between strong and weak inventory.
Note any case where clearing prices differ widely but the floor stays the same, which points to inventory that a segmented floor could price more accurately.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the floor values across placements, formats, geographies, and device types alongside the average clearing price for each. Floors that follow the value differences the reporting shows, with higher floors on inventory that clears higher and lower floors on inventory that clears lower, pass. A single flat floor applied across inventory whose clearing prices differ widely is a fail recorded with the dimensions where the floor and the value diverge.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the floor values and the average clearing price by placement, format, geography, and device above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} applies a single flat floor across inventory whose value differs by placement, format, geography, and device. A flat floor either underprices the strong inventory, leaving revenue uncollected, or overprices the weak inventory, leaving impressions unsold, so one number cannot fit inventory that clears at different prices. {{voice_recommend}} reading the average clearing price by placement, format, geography, and device in Microsoft Monetize, then setting floor rules that carry higher floors on the inventory that clears higher and lower floors on the inventory that clears lower. After the change, recheck fill rate and average price across the segments to confirm the floors fit the value of each.

audit_checks.title: Dynamic floors are accepted without evaluating them against a fixed baseline
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Dynamic floors adjust per auction to balance fill and value, which often raises revenue, but the optimization objective does not always match a publisher's strategy, for example protecting a premium price point. Accepting a dynamic floor without comparing it to a fixed-floor baseline leaves the choice untested, so a publisher cannot tell whether the dynamic floor is helping or trading away inventory value on a slice of inventory.

educate.base:
A floor in Microsoft Monetize can be set in more than one way. A fixed floor is a number the publisher chooses and the platform applies as written. A dynamic floor instead adjusts per auction, often supplied by a floor-optimization service that reads recent bid behavior and moves the floor to find the price that fills the impression without giving the inventory away. Because Microsoft Monetize is Prebid-compatible, dynamic floors here follow the same pattern the Prebid Price Floors module documents, where floor data is fetched in real time and refreshed separately rather than written once by hand.

The risk is not that dynamic floors are wrong. The risk is accepting one without a comparison. A dynamic floor optimizes toward the objective it was built for, which is yield across the pool, and that objective does not always match a publisher's own strategy. A publisher protecting a premium price point on a sponsorship-adjacent placement, or holding a deliberate floor to keep a direct-sold rate intact, may find the dynamic floor clears impressions below the price they meant to protect. Without a baseline to measure against, the publisher has no way to see that happening.

The comparison is straightforward to run. A publisher can apply the dynamic floor to a slice of inventory, hold a fixed floor on a comparable slice, and read the revenue, fill rate, and average clearing price across both. That turns the floor choice into a measured result rather than a default that was never questioned. The fixed baseline does not have to stay in place forever. It only has to run long enough to produce a fair read against the dynamic floor.

A few signs point to a dynamic floor being accepted rather than evaluated:
  • A dynamic or vendor-supplied floor runs across all inventory with no fixed-floor comparison on record
  • No experiment or before-and-after read exists showing how the dynamic floor performed against a fixed baseline
  • The publisher cannot say why the dynamic option was chosen beyond it being the vendor's recommendation or the default
You may want to confirm that the dynamic-versus-fixed choice was made on evidence rather than by default. One approach is to hold a fixed-floor baseline on part of the inventory long enough to compare it against the dynamic floor on the rest.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, premium placements such as a homepage takeover slot or a sponsorship-adjacent unit often carry a price the publisher set on purpose to protect direct-sold value. You may want to keep a fixed-floor baseline on those units while running the dynamic floor elsewhere, so the comparison shows whether the dynamic floor respects the premium or clears below it.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, product and category pages can command higher prices than editorial pages because the audience is closer to a purchase. You may want to compare the dynamic floor against a fixed floor on the commercial pages specifically, rather than judging it on a blended average that the lower-value editorial inventory pulls down.

learn_more.source_title: Price Floors for Header Bidding (dynamic floors fetched and refreshed in real time)
OpenRTB (bidfloor signaling between seller and buyer)

educate.learn_more_note: Microsoft Monetize's detailed configuration documentation for dynamic and fixed floor settings is in the Microsoft Advertising partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review which floors run dynamic versus fixed, and look for any test result or before-and-after read that compares them.

monetize_console.label: Microsoft Monetize console
steps: Open the seller's floor configuration and note, for each floor in place, whether it is a fixed value or a dynamic, vendor-supplied floor.
Record which slices of inventory run on a dynamic floor and which, if any, run on a fixed floor that could serve as a comparison baseline.
Ask the publisher whether any experiment or before-and-after comparison was run, and record what the result showed and when it was measured.

monetize_reporting.label: Microsoft Monetize reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by the floor type or the inventory it targets, with revenue, impressions, fill rate, and average eCPM.
Compare a slice running a dynamic floor against a comparable slice on a fixed floor, if one exists, to see whether the dynamic floor raised revenue or cleared at a lower price.
Note whether the data supports the current floor choice or whether no fixed-floor slice exists to compare against.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per floor in place, whether it is fixed or dynamic, and whether any comparison between a dynamic floor and a fixed baseline exists with a result on record. A setup where the dynamic-versus-fixed choice is deliberate and backed by a comparison passes. A setup where dynamic floors are applied across inventory with no fixed-floor comparison and no stated reason for the choice is a fail recorded with the floors and inventory affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the floor type for each floor and whether a dynamic-versus-fixed comparison exists above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} runs a dynamic floor without a fixed-floor comparison on record, so the choice between a dynamic and a fixed floor has not been tested. A dynamic floor in Microsoft Monetize adjusts to maximize yield across the pool, which often helps, but that objective does not always match a publisher's own strategy, such as protecting a premium price point, and without a baseline the difference goes unmeasured. {{voice_recommend}} holding a fixed-floor baseline on a slice of comparable inventory, running the dynamic floor on the rest, and comparing revenue, fill rate, and average price across both before settling on a floor strategy. After the comparison, keep whichever floor the data supports for each slice of inventory.
